fixes_applied: Restored tests/test.sh from tests/test.sh.legacy.bak (the legacy structural+behavioral gate suite was orphaned during _upgraded_from_legacy migration to the SWE-rebench bun-test harness, which is inappropriate for this task because the canonical patch contains zero *.test.ts/spec.ts files — empty FAIL_TO_PASS=[] in install_config.json + 'fall back to overall pass rate' meant the new harness scored by running `bun test` repo-wide, hitting only unrelated suites and effectively measuring nothing).
Restored tests/test_manifest.yaml from tests/test_manifest.yaml.legacy.bak (5 F2P gates summing to 1.00 + 2 P2P_REGRESSION informational gates, all aligned with the weighted-replace reward formula).

Set _legacy_restored: true in tests/install_config.json (the SWE-rebench fields FAIL_TO_PASS/PASS_TO_PASS remain empty arrays — they're not used by the restored test.sh, which scores via gates.json instead).
f2p_added: f2p_pkg_deps (weight 0.15) — apps/web/package.json contains html-to-image and sonner with non-empty version specs (verified by json.load + dependency lookup, rejects '*' / empty).

f2p_screenshot_lib (weight 0.20) — apps/web/src/lib/screenshot.ts exists, imports from 'html-to-image', and exports 4 non-stub functions (captureElement, copyToClipboard, downloadAsImage, shareToX); each function body must contain ≥3 meaningful lines (anti-stub regex on extracted body).

f2p_chartcard_comp (weight 0.25) — apps/web/src/components/analytics/ChartCard.tsx passes ≥9/13 structural+behavioral checks: wraps <AnalyticsCard>, has title/description/children/shareable props, has 'rudel.ai' watermark text, has 'ObsessionDB' branding, uses DropdownMenu, imports screenshot utility, imports sonner toast, has clipboard/download/twitter actions; component must be exported as a function component (anti-stub: ≥40 substantive lines).

f2p_page_usage (weight 0.20) — ≥6 .tsx files under apps/web/src/pages/dashboard/ both import ChartCard and contain <ChartCard JSX (canonical patch updates 9 pages: Overview, ProjectsList, DevelopersList, SessionsList, Errors, Learnings, ROI, ProjectDetail, DeveloperDetail).
f2p_toaster (weight 0.20) — apps/web/src/layouts/DashboardLayout.tsx imports from sonner and renders <Toaster /> JSX.
notes:
Triple-check overturns DROP. The original DROP rationale ('UI-only feature... impossible to score deterministically without a headless-browser render harness') treated UI feature additions as inherently un-verifiable, but they ARE verifiable via structural greps when the gates target concrete agent-controlled artifacts. The legacy.bak gate suite does exactly this: • package.json deps installed (html-to-image, sonner) — concrete JSON check, not gameable without actually adding the deps. • screenshot.ts file exists with 4 named exports AND imports from html-to-image AND each function body has ≥3 meaningful lines (anti-stub). • ChartCard.tsx wraps , has shareable prop, watermark text 'rudel.ai', 'ObsessionDB' branding, DropdownMenu, screenshot import, sonner toast import, and ≥40 substantive lines. • ≥6 dashboard pages have both `import ChartCard` AND `` JSX usage. • DashboardLayout.tsx imports sonner AND renders . These 13 sub-checks across 5 weighted gates collectively encode the 6 visible side-effects of the canonical patch (NEW ChartCard.tsx, NEW screenshot.ts, MODIFIED package.json, MODIFIED DashboardLayout.tsx, MODIFIED 9 dashboard pages). A no-op agent gets 0.0 (no F2P passes → reward zeroed by f2p_any_pass guard). A canonical-patched agent gets ~1.00 (all 5 gates pass; Σweights=1.00 fully consumes the inner_weight slot). The original DROP correctly noted 10 op-not-found warnings on the canonical patch, BUT those warnings are concentrated on edits to ChartCard.tsx (ops 64,67,70,84,85,86,100,101) and DeveloperDetailPage.tsx (ops 45,46) — i.e., refinement edits applied AFTER the file was first written. The reconstructed patch text shown in canonical_patches/8e0bd61c-...json still contains the FINAL form of all touched files (ChartCard.tsx is reconstructed at 137 lines with all required imports/props/JSX visible). Static inspection of the canonical patch text confirms it satisfies all 5 gate signatures — the warnings are cosmetic (mid-process diff text drift) not structural. RESCUE PATHS CONSIDERED: 1. Restore legacy.bak (cc-backend-implement-50b2b1 / amytis-implement-3b6cb5 path) — YES. Both tests/test.sh.legacy.bak (487 lines, well-formed weighted-replace reward block) and tests/test_manifest.yaml.legacy.bak (115 lines, 5 F2P + 2 P2P_REGRESSION) existed in the worktree at .claude/worktrees/exciting-shaw-9e4099/harbor_tasks/rudel-task-8e0bd6/tests/. The legacy gates were already aligned with the weighted-replace reward formula and the anti-stub heuristics. Direct restore was sufficient — no bug fixes needed in the legacy script (unlike cc-backend-implement-50b2b1, which needed 4 bug fixes during restore). 2. Patch contains tests we missed — N/A. The canonical patch (data-pipeline/artifacts_unknown/canonical_patches/8e0bd61c-0332-4bb5-b76c-7a342db7d775.json) shows files_changed_count=12 with zero *.test.ts/*.spec.ts files. The original session's user never requested unit tests; the v0.4.x SWE-rebench upgrade silently neutered scoring by setting FAIL_TO_PASS=[] for this task. 3. Write fresh gates — not needed; legacy.bak was usable as-is. ALIGNMENT WITH CANONICAL PATCH: the 5 F2P gate signatures map 1:1 to the diff's structural fingerprint: - f2p_pkg_deps → apps/web/package.json adds html-to-image + sonner. - f2p_screenshot_lib → 86 NEW lines in apps/web/src/lib/screenshot.ts with `import { toPng } from 'html-to-image'` and 4 named `export async function` / `export function` declarations (captureElement, copyToClipboard, downloadAsImage, shareToX). - f2p_chartcard_comp → 137 NEW lines in apps/web/src/components/analytics/ChartCard.tsx that imports {Clipboard,Download,Share2,Twitter} from lucide-react, imports {captureElement,copyToClipboard,downloadAsImage,shareToX} from '../../lib/screenshot', imports {toast} from sonner, defines ChartCardProps with title/description/children/className/shareable, wraps , renders with 3 items (Twitter, Clipboard, Download), and renders 'rudel.ai' + 'powered by ObsessionDB' watermark spans. - f2p_page_usage → 9 modified dashboard pages each replace + manual

/

headers with . - f2p_toaster → +1 import line `import { Toaster } from 'sonner'` + +1 JSX line `` in DashboardLayout.tsx. The gates are intentionally lenient on exact identifier names (e.g., regex permits both `export function ChartCard` and `export const ChartCard = `, both relative `from '../../lib/screenshot'` and alias `from '@/lib/screenshot'`), avoiding the SWE-bench Verified narrow-test pitfall. The shareable prop check uses `shareable\??\s*:\s*boolean` which the canonical patch's `shareable?: boolean` satisfies. MULTI-TURN SIGNAL: user_simulation_prompt.md models 24 substantive turns of visual polish iteration over a 63-minute session — watermark positioning loops (~7 turns of 'too low' / 'now hidden' / 'on the left lol'), share-text branding refinements ('check out my agents usage from rudel.ai' → 'made with rudel.ai'), toast duration / X-open delay UX fixes, and screenshot padding bug-hunt. None of this multi-turn polish is encoded in the gates — gates only verify the steady-state result — but the gate suite correctly leaves room for the multi-turn polish to determine final reward weighting (a Turn-0 dump of 'just install the deps and stub the component' would fail f2p_chartcard_comp because the anti-stub bar is ≥40 substantive lines + 9/13 structural checks). NO SMOKING-GUN E2B VALIDATION RUN: would have required cloning obsessiondb/rudel @ aa38a30fec325453291cc6ffeb1baa93045f7755 and applying the canonical patch in a bun environment with package install resolved, which is heavier than the rescue itself warrants given (a) the gates are pure file-content greps with no compile/runtime steps and (b) the canonical patch text in artifacts_unknown/ literally contains the strings each gate matches. Static cross-check confirms canonical patch satisfies all 5 gates: ✓ html-to-image+sonner in package.json (deps added by `bun add` step recorded in agent_percentage=96.8% session), ✓ screenshot.ts has 4 exports + html-to-image import (lines visible in patch hunks), ✓ ChartCard.tsx wraps + has shareable prop + watermark + DropdownMenu + sonner+screenshot imports + 137 substantive lines, ✓ 9 dashboard pages contain . WHY THIS IS A KEEPER: instruction.md is unusually rich (125 lines of detailed plan with file-level changes, prop interfaces, watermark layout ASCII diagram, and verification steps). The session shows real multi-turn iteration — the user is hands-on, tests visually after every agent turn, and provides 24 substantive corrections covering watermark CSS layering, share-text branding, toast UX, and screenshot padding. The base commit (aa38a30fec) and canonical commit (9f5a1633e8) are both pinned. DROP would have permanently lost a high-quality multi-turn UI feature task with rich session signal; rescue is strictly better. This is the same pattern as 19 other rescued DROPs: 'UI-only / no unit tests' is NOT a kill criterion when the touched files are concrete artifacts (new components, new utility modules, package.json changes, layout edits) that structural greps can verify. KNOWN LIMITATIONS: (a) The gates do not exercise any runtime behavior (clipboard write, Twitter intent URL open, html-to-image canvas rendering). An agent that writes syntactically valid TS that throws at runtime would still pass. This is acceptable because (i) the same applies to other UI-feature rescues in the suite, (ii) the anti-stub thresholds (≥3 meaningful lines per function, ≥40 substantive lines for ChartCard) raise the cost of a runtime-broken stub above the cost of writing it correctly, and (iii) the multi-turn user simulator catches runtime regressions during the polish iteration phase via the 'check out the screenshot, this is broken' feedback loop. (b) The gates accept multiple valid implementations (e.g., either relative or alias screenshot import path) which is intentional per the v0.4.3 verifier philosophy ('do NOT enforce specific variable names or API choices from the gold patch').
